{"step_by_step_thinking": "The question asks whether instrumental activities of daily living (IADLs) predict dementia at 1- and 2-year follow-up. Reviewing the documents, several key points emerge: Document [2] states that 'individuals with MCI who have impairments performing IADLs are more likely to develop dementia.' This directly links IADL impairments to the development of dementia. Document [8] notes that 'instrumental activities of daily living such as driving, financial management, cooking, and detailed activity planning are affected relatively early in their dementia.' Document [15] emphasizes that assessing IADLs provides valuable insights into cognitive and functional status, and deficits in IADLs are associated with cognitive decline. Document [26] states that about 15% to 20% of people aged 65 or older have mild cognitive impairment, and approximately one-third of them develop Alzheimer disease within 5 years of follow-up, suggesting a predictive relationship between functional decline and dementia progression. While some documents note limitations in IADL instruments (e.g., self-report bias, lack of sensitivity to subtle changes), the overall body of evidence supports that impairments in IADLs are associated with and predictive of dementia development over time, including at 1- and 2-year follow-ups. Therefore, the evidence supports a 'yes' answer.", "answer_choice": "A"}
